title: Key Management Operations from HSM and KMS Audit Trails
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the PCI DSS programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with PCI DSS control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 3.6.1 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 3.6.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.15: Key Management Operations from HSM and KMS Audit Trails.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.11.15' running on sourcetype aws:cloudtrail and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Key Management Operations from HSM and KMS Audit Trails' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper PCI DSS control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the PCI DSS control objective.

app: Splunk Add-on for AWS (1876), custom HSM syslog parser (Splunkbase)
spl:
(index=aws sourcetype="aws:cloudtrail" eventSource="kms.amazonaws.com" earliest=-24h)
 OR (index=hsm sourcetype="thales:luna:audit" earliest=-24h)
| eval op=coalesce(eventName, hsm_command, operation)
| where match(op,"(?i)delete|disable|import|unwrap|clone|partition")
| stats count by user, op, src_ip, host
| sort - count
description: Centralizes HSM partition and cloud KMS administrative events so cryptographic key custodians can prove dual-control and logging for keys that protect stored account data.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for PCI DSS compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes aws, hsm and sourcetypes aws:cloudtrail, thales:luna:audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Map IAM user/role to human custodian IDs. (2) Require ticket ID in CloudTrail session context where possible. (3) Alert on `ScheduleKeyDeletion` without CAB record. (4) Retain 12+ months in compliant storage tier. Indexes required: `index=aws`, `index=hsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail`, `sourcetype=thales:luna:audit`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection.Intrusion_Detection by Intrusion_Detection.signature, Intrusion_Detection.dest, Intrusion_Detection.severity | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Intrusion_Detection (CIM data model).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised penetration testing** — Annual or more frequent penetration tests conducted by QSA-approved assessors against cardholder data environment (CDE) systems produce expected security alerts that are pre-communicated and tracked via the test engagement letter.

2. **PCI ASV scanning activity** — Quarterly external vulnerability scans performed by Approved Scanning Vendors generate network probe events that trigger IDS/IPS alerts as an expected part of the PCI DSS validation process.

3. **Tokenization system batch processing** — The payment tokenization platform performs scheduled batch re-tokenization and token lifecycle management operations that produce high-volume cardholder data access patterns within approved operational windows.

4. **Acquirer and processor settlement activity** — End-of-day settlement reconciliation between the merchant platform and payment processor involves legitimate PAN/SAD data transmission that is covered by the processor service provider agreement.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `pci_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved pen-test engagement IDs, ASV scan source IPs, scheduled batch job names, and settlement processing windows. The search filters matches where `pci_exception_valid=true` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase app ID 1876) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for AWS" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `aws`, `hsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `3.6.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI DSS 3.6.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.15: Key Management Operations from HSM and KMS Audit Trails." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase app ID 1876), landing events in `aws`, `hsm` under `aws:cloudtrail`, `thales:luna:audit`. CloudTrail management events average 5000-50000 API calls/account/hour for active workloads (~5-50 GB/account/month). Data events (S3, Lambda) can multiply this 10-100x.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > CloudTrail (SQS-based ingest is strongly preferred over S3 polling). A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[aws_cloudtrail://prod_account]
aws_account = production
aws_region = us-east-1
sqs_queue = splunk-ct-queue
index = aws
sourcetype = aws:cloudtrail
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `eventName, eventSource, userIdentity.arn, sourceIPAddress, requestParameters, responseElements, errorCode, userAgent`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=aws earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Map IAM user/role to human custodian IDs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Require ticket ID in CloudTrail session context where possible. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Alert on `ScheduleKeyDeletion` without CAB record. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Retain 12+ months in compliant storage tier. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `count()` across the last 24 hours, grouped by `user, op, src_ip, host`. For "Key Management Operations from HSM and KMS Audit Trails," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the PCI DSS control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `user, op, src_ip, host` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(user) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Intrusion_Detection` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection.Intrusion_Detection by Intrusion_Detection.signature, Intrusion_Detection.dest, Intrusion_Detection.severity | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Intrusion_Detection` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.11.15: Key Management Operations from HSM and KMS Audit Trails]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.11.15,reg=PCI_DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=aws sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `host`, `op`, `src_ip`, `user`. Run:

```spl
index=aws sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table host, op, src_ip, user
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.11.15: PCI DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Time trend (`operations`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`custodian`, `op`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Pie chart (`op mix`):** Shows proportion across categorical dimension. Use sparingly — pie charts are poor at small-slice comparison; a bar chart is often clearer.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We centralizes HSM partition and cloud KMS administrative events so cryptographic key custodians can prove dual-control and logging for keys that protect stored account data. PCI DSS is about protecting card and payment data when we run these checks.
